description: Real-world insurer denial patterns sourced from published research. Used by p4_realistic_flaw_injector to embed exploitable flaws into synthetic denial letters. Each pattern maps to one or more appeal vectors that the Aegis agent must discover.

patterns.description: Insurer cites MCG guidelines without naming the specific edition or module
patterns.source: KFF 2023 Survey of Consumer Experiences with Health Insurance
patterns.appeal_vector: Request the specific criteria used; insurer often cannot produce them

patterns.description: Denial letter omits some or all legally required ERISA disclosure rights
patterns.source: DOL EBSA Technical Release 2011-01; common in Aetna pre-2024 templates
patterns.appeal_vector: Procedural violation under 29 U.S.C. § 1133

patterns.description: Denial fails to state clearly whether the patient is financially liable if they proceed with the treatment.
patterns.source: No Surprises Act Compliance Audit 2024
patterns.appeal_vector: Use as leverage if treatment was already rendered; argue patient wasn't properly informed of liability.

patterns.description: Denial letter omits the right to independent external review (IRO), which is legally required under ACA for non-grandfathered plans.
patterns.source: CMS External Appeal Regulations 45 CFR § 147.136; ACA § 2719
patterns.appeal_vector: Procedural defect — insurer must re-issue denial with proper IRO notice, effectively restarting the appeal clock.

patterns.description: Denial issued past the regulatory deadline — 72 hours for urgent/concurrent care, 30 days for standard pre-service.
patterns.source: DOL ERISA Claims Procedure Regulations 29 CFR § 2560.503-1; documented in UHC state AG complaints 2022–2024
patterns.appeal_vector: Procedural deemed-denial: if insurer missed deadline, the claim may be treated as approved by operation of law.

patterns.description: Denial letter lists incorrect or outdated appeals department address, fax, or portal, making timely appeal submission harder.
patterns.source: State Insurance Commissioner complaint logs (CA, NY, TX) 2023
realistic_flaws: wrong fax number for appeals submission
patterns.appeal_vector: If appeal was submitted to the listed address and rejected as 'wrong department', use the defective notice as grounds to extend the appeal deadline.

patterns.description: Denial states 'not medically necessary' because it 'does not meet criteria for medical necessity', offering no clinical specifics.
patterns.source: ProPublica 'Uncovered' Series 2023
patterns.appeal_vector: Highlight lack of specific clinical rationale; force disclosure of the actual threshold.

patterns.description: Applies a guideline meant for adults to a pediatric case, or vice versa.
patterns.source: Commonwealth Fund 2023 Review of Prior Auth Denials
patterns.appeal_vector: Cite peer-reviewed evidence that the insurer's cited guideline is inapplicable to this patient's age group.

patterns.description: Denial template completely ignores the letter of medical necessity previously submitted by the physician.
patterns.source: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey
realistic_flaws: makes no reference to submitted evidence
patterns.appeal_vector: Point out the ignored evidence; demand a peer-to-peer review since the initial reviewer clearly didn't read the file.

patterns.description: Insurer cites a clinical guideline or coverage policy that has since been withdrawn, superseded, or updated to support coverage.
patterns.source: AHIP 2022 Coverage Policy Review; documented in multiple Cigna litigation cases 2021–2023
patterns.appeal_vector: Produce the current version of the cited guideline to show it now supports coverage; argue insurer's medical policy is out of date.

patterns.description: Insurer demands the patient try a first-line alternative that the patient's records document as contraindicated or previously failed.
patterns.source: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey; State step-therapy exception laws (NY, TX, IL)
realistic_flaws: demands step therapy despite documented allergy or adverse reaction in chart
patterns.appeal_vector: Cite documented contraindication or prior failure directly from clinical notes; invoke state step-therapy exception law if applicable.

patterns.description: The denial was issued by a reviewer who is not board-certified in the relevant specialty — particularly common and legally significant in behavioral health under MHPAEA.
patterns.source: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024 (29 CFR § 2590.712); Cal. Health & Safety Code § 1374.72
realistic_flaws: MD specialty not matching clinical area
patterns.appeal_vector: Request the reviewer's credentials and specialty; if non-matching, the denial review was procedurally defective and must be repeated by a qualified specialist.

patterns.description: Insurer applies a stricter annual visit limit to mental health/SUD treatment than to comparable medical or surgical benefits.
patterns.source: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024; KFF 2023 Mental Health Parity Enforcement Report
realistic_flaws: caps behavioral health at 30 visits/year while no cap exists for comparable medical benefit
visit limit not disclosed in plain language
patterns.appeal_vector: Document the asymmetry by identifying the analogous medical/surgical benefit with no visit cap; cite MHPAEA's quantitative treatment limitation (QTL) parity requirement.

patterns.description: Insurer requires fail-first step therapy for a behavioral health treatment but does not impose equivalent step therapy requirements for a comparable medical/surgical condition.
patterns.source: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024; DOL MHPAEA Self-Compliance Tool 2023
realistic_flaws: demands 6-month outpatient trial before IOP approval
no analogous requirement exists for medical inpatient step-down
patterns.appeal_vector: Invoke MHPAEA non-quantitative treatment limitation (NQTL) parity: if insurer cannot show an analogous step-therapy requirement for medical/surgical benefits, the restriction is unlawful.

patterns.description: Insurer uses more restrictive level-of-care criteria to approve psychiatric inpatient or IOP admission than it uses for medically comparable inpatient or partial hospitalization.
patterns.source: MHPAEA Final Rule 2024; Wit v. United Behavioral Health (9th Cir. 2023)
realistic_flaws: applies UBH Level of Care Guidelines instead of generally accepted clinical standards
uses proprietary criteria more restrictive than APA/ASAM
patterns.appeal_vector: Cite Wit v. UBH and the MHPAEA Final Rule: insurer must use generally accepted standards of care (APA, ASAM), not internally developed criteria that are more restrictive.

patterns.description: Insurer labels a treatment 'experimental or investigational' using an outdated coverage policy that predates the relevant FDA approval or updated NCCN/AHA guideline.
patterns.source: ProPublica 'Uncovered' Series 2023; ASCO 2023 Coverage Policy Analysis
realistic_flaws: coverage policy last reviewed 2+ years ago
FDA approval or NCCN Category 1 designation not reflected
patterns.appeal_vector: Produce the current FDA label or NCCN guideline showing Category 1 evidence; demonstrate insurer's coverage policy is stale and its own stated review cycle has lapsed.

patterns.description: Service is placed in the wrong benefit category — e.g., speech therapy for a stroke survivor coded as 'habilitative' (excluded) rather than 'rehabilitative' (covered).
patterns.source: CMS Essential Health Benefits Bulletin 2022; documented in Aetna member complaints
patterns.appeal_vector: Cite the clinical definition of rehabilitation (restoring lost function) vs. habilitation (developing new function); provide physician letter confirming the correct classification.

patterns.description: Denial invokes a plan exclusion that conflicts with a binding state insurance mandate (e.g., state mandated mental health coverage, infertility, or autism ABA therapy).
patterns.source: NCSL State Health Insurance Mandates Database 2024; documented in CA, NJ, IL enforcement actions
realistic_flaws: cites plan exclusion without checking state mandate applicability
applies self-funded plan logic to a fully-insured state-regulated plan
patterns.appeal_vector: Identify the applicable state mandate and confirm the plan is fully-insured (not ERISA self-funded); state mandates preempt conflicting plan exclusions for fully-insured plans.

patterns.description: Insurer requests additional clinical documentation but gives an unreasonably short response window (e.g., 48 hours) with no extension option, effectively manufacturing a denial.
patterns.source: DOL ERISA Claims Procedure Regulations 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(g); AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Survey
realistic_flaws: 48-hour doc request with no extension language
request sent to provider portal with no fax/phone backup
patterns.appeal_vector: Argue the documentation request was procedurally defective; a 48-hour window is not a 'reasonable' opportunity to submit information under ERISA regulations.

patterns.description: Insurer closes an active appeal as 'member withdrawn' without documentation that the member ever withdrew, effectively ending the appeal clock.
patterns.source: State Insurance Commissioner complaint logs (CA DOI 2022, NY DFS 2023)
realistic_flaws: closure letter cites withdrawal not requested by member
no written withdrawal confirmation on file
patterns.appeal_vector: Demand the insurer produce written evidence of withdrawal; if none exists, the appeal was wrongly closed and the clock must be restarted.

patterns.description: Insurer communicates the peer-to-peer review window verbally (by phone to office staff) but does not confirm in writing, making it impossible for the physician to verify or dispute the window.
patterns.source: AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Physician Survey; documented in multiple state PA reform laws (TX HB 2002, VA SB 1272)
realistic_flaws: window communicated to staff not treating physician
patterns.appeal_vector: Argue the peer-to-peer opportunity was not meaningfully provided; request a new P2P window in writing and cite the applicable state PA reform statute if available.
